title: Eton mess
subtitle: Crushed meringue, whipped cream and strawberries
excerpt: Eton mess is equal parts crisp meringue, whipped cream and fresh strawberries, broken together in a bowl. The meringues can be made a day ahead; the assembly takes five minutes. No slicing, no piping, no precision required.
sourceNotes: Eton mess is a British dessert traditionally served at the Eton College cricket match against Harrow, documented in its current form from the 1930s. The name is older; early references describe a mixed fruit and cream dish. The meringue version became the standard British interpretation in the post-war era. The recipe here follows the established British formula.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the meringues a day ahead and store in an airtight container. Make the strawberry compote an hour ahead. Assemble just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: for the meringues; conventional oven only

glossaryTerms.definition: Maceration means leaving cut fruit with sugar for 20 to 30 minutes to draw out the juices. For Eton mess, macerating half the strawberries with a teaspoon of sugar produces a concentrated, jammy base that coats the meringue and cream. The other half go in fresh for texture contrast. The syrup left in the bowl is poured over at the end.

content.text: The secret to good Eton mess is contrast: some meringue should stay in larger pieces to give a satisfying crunch, while some shatters to small fragments that absorb into the cream. The same principle applies to the strawberries: half mashed and
with sugar, the other half left in halves for texture. Fold everything together loosely just before serving.

Heat the oven to 100 degrees C (conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the 3 egg whites and a quarter teaspoon cream of tartar to soft peaks, then add the 150 g caster sugar one tablespoon at a time, whisking on high until thick and glossy. Spoon into 8 to 10 rough blobs on the tray. Bake for 1 hour 30 minutes until dry and crisp throughout, then cool in the oven with the door ajar. The meringues can be stored in an airtight tin for up to 3 days.
Halve the 500 g strawberries. Crush half of them with a fork in a bowl and stir in the 1 tsp caster sugar. Leave for 20 minutes. Set the rest aside whole.

Whip the 350 ml double cream to soft peaks. Break the meringues into rough pieces. Fold together the cream, meringue pieces, crushed strawberries and their juice, and the halved strawberries, leaving large pockets of each visible. Divide between four glasses or a large bowl and serve immediately.

items.solution: The meringues were not fully dry, or the mess was assembled too far ahead. Meringues must be completely crisp, not just dry on the outside. Assemble no more than 10 to 15 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Cream turns watery at the bottom
items.solution: The cream was over-whipped to stiff peaks rather than soft, then the strawberry juice thinned it. Whip only to soft, billowy peaks. The juice from the macerated berries will fold in and loosen the cream to the right consistency.
